n: Catacumbas del Monasterio de San Francisco
hook: Bone-stacked colonial ossuary beneath a Franciscan convent
facts: Built from 1546 as crypts beneath the Basilica and Convent of San Francisco
Used as a cemetery until 1808, with an estimated 25,000 bodies
Skulls and femurs sorted into geometric patterns in circular wells
Opened as a museum; entrance off the Plazuela San Francisco
w: Friars dug crypts under the convent in 1546 to outlast earthquakes, then buried their dead here for 260 years until 1808, some 25,000 of them. Below the gilded cloisters, brick wells brim with skulls and femurs sorted into careful rings. The city's first cemetery, stacked by hand.

hook: Seven-tiered adobe pyramid stranded in a modern district
facts: Ceremonial and administrative centre of the Lima culture, roughly 200-700 AD
Built from millions of hand-made adobe bricks set vertically like books
Stands 22 metres high amid Miraflores apartment blocks
Later used as a Wari burial ground for nobility
w: Around 500 AD the Lima culture raised this clay mountain from millions of small adobe bricks, set upright like books on a shelf so quakes could shudder through the gaps. Seven platforms, 22 metres tall, now ringed by Miraflores traffic. A pyramid the city simply grew up around.

hook: Restored stepped pyramid hemmed in by mansions
facts: Begun by the Huallas around 200 BC, reused into Inca times
Truncated three-level pyramid roughly 20 metres high
1958 dig found 48 funerary bundles at the summit cemetery
Its small site museum was among the first of its kind in Peru
w: Once called Pan de Azucar, sugarloaf, for its smoothed silhouette, this stepped mound rose from 200 BC and served as ceremony and grave for 1,500 years. In 1958 diggers pulled 48 mummy bundles from its crown. Now it sits walled in by San Isidro's smartest houses, faintly absurd.

hook: Wooden bridge with a breath-holding wish ritual
facts: Wooden footbridge over the Bajada de Banos ravine, 44 metres long
Built in 1876, wrecked in the War of the Pacific, rebuilt 1883
Named for Chabuca Granda's 1960 song El Puente de los Suspiros
Links Ayacucho street with the Paseo Chabuca Granda
legend: Legend says crossing the bridge in one held breath grants a wish, often a wish for love
w: Forty-four metres of creaking timber over a leafy ravine, built in 1876 and patched back together after the War of the Pacific flattened it. Legend says fill your lungs at one end and cross without breathing and your wish, usually for love, comes true. Chabuca Granda set it to song in 1960.

hook: Latin America's first municipal cemetery, a forest of marble
facts: Opened 1808, the first municipal cemetery in Latin America
Declared a museum in 1999 for its sculpture and architecture
Holds 766 mausoleums and 92 historic monuments
Includes the Crypt of the Heroes of the War of the Pacific
w: Opened in 1808 as Latin America's first municipal cemetery, named for the priest-architect who laid it out. Locals call it a marble forest: 766 mausoleums and Carrara angels crowd the avenues, sheltering presidents, poets and the heroes of the Pacific war. A museum of the dead since 1999.

hook: Modernist necropolis behind a black-granite colonnade
facts: Inaugurated in 1959 when Presbitero Maestro ran out of room
Entrance mural by painter Fernando de Szyszlo, sculpture by Joaquin Roca Rey
Colonnade revetted in black granite imported from Sweden
Some 600,000 bodies rest across 640 pavilions
w: When Presbitero Maestro filled, the city opened this one in 1959, fronting it with a colonnade clad in Swedish black granite, a Szyszlo mural and a Roca Rey angel, modernist Peru at full volume. Behind it, 640 pavilions and some 600,000 dead in stacked concrete. Grief, designed.

hook: Convent holding the relics of three Peruvian saints
facts: Founded in the 1530s on land granted at Lima's founding
Tomb of Santa Rosa de Lima sits in a crypt below the chapter house
Skulls of Santa Rosa and San Martin de Porres shown in a glass shrine
San Martin, born here in 1579, was the Americas' first mixed-race saint
w: Three saints rest behind these pink walls: Rosa de Lima, who gave her body to the friars in 1617, San Juan Macias, and San Martin de Porres, born nearby in 1579 and the Americas' first mixed-race saint. The skulls of Rosa and Martin sit in glass beside the altar. A reliquary you can walk into.

hook: Inquisition tribunal with its dungeons and torture cellar
facts: Housed the Holy Office tribunal in Lima from 1570 to 1820
Underground cells and a torture chamber survive beneath the building
Later home to Peru's Senate; now a free museum by Plaza Bolivar
Carved coffered ceiling of the main hall is original
w: From 1570 to 1820 the Holy Office judged heretics and 'witches' from this house off Plaza Bolivar, sentencing some to burn in the square outside. Down a stair wait the holding cells and a dim torture room laid out with wax figures mid-ordeal. Above it all, a serenely beautiful carved ceiling.

hook: The same family's home since Lima was founded in 1535
facts: Granted to conquistador Jeronimo de Aliaga at the 1535 founding
Occupied by his descendants for some seventeen generations
Built atop a pre-Hispanic shrine just off the Plaza Mayor
Visits by reservation only, as it remains a private residence
w: When Pizarro carved up Lima in 1535 he handed this plot to his lieutenant Jeronimo de Aliaga, whose descendants never left. Seventeen-odd generations on, the Aliagas still live behind the gilt salons and cedar balconies, atop a buried Inca shrine. Knock, with a reservation, and history answers.

hook: Pre-Columbian erotic pottery in a vice-regal mansion
facts: Pre-Columbian art museum set in an 18th-century building on a 7th-century pyramid
Holds some 30,000 catalogued ceramic vessels
Separate Checan gallery devoted to Moche erotic ceramics
Founded by Rafael Larco Herrera in 1926
w: A whitewashed colonial mansion, itself perched on a 7th-century pyramid, guards 30,000 ancient pots. Most visitors make for a side gallery: the Checan room, where Moche potters fired startlingly frank scenes of sex 1,500 years ago. Pre-Columbian Peru, with nothing left to the imagination.

hook: Faded grand hotel famous for its Catedral pisco sour
facts: Opened 1924 as Lima's first large modern hotel, on Plaza San Martin
Designed by architect Rafael Marquina y Bueno
Bar known for the oversized Catedral pisco sour
Past guests said to include Ava Gardner, Orson Welles and the Rolling Stones
w: Lima's first grand hotel, thrown up on Plaza San Martin in 1924, where presidents and Hollywood once swept under the stained-glass dome. The glamour has worn thin, but the corner bar still pours the Catedral, a pisco sour the size of a small font. Order one, toast the ghosts, mind the steps.

hook: Yellow corner house wrapped in haunting legends
facts: Stands at Avenida Garcilaso de la Vega and Espana in central Lima
Upper floors have long sat empty while shops trade below
A TV presenter reputedly spent a night inside and was hospitalised
Once faced the United States embassy across the avenue
legend: Legend says a wronged servant poisoned a banquet here, leaving the rooms cursed
They say one man who dared a night inside emerged raving
w: Shops bustle on the ground floor; the storeys above stay shuttered and still. Legend says a servant once poisoned a banquet here and the rooms never recovered, and they say a TV host who braved a night inside came out raving. Probably just an empty flat. Lima's most stared-at window all the same.

hook: Barefoot friars' retreat at the end of a tree-lined promenade
facts: Franciscan recollection house founded in 1595 below Cerro San Cristobal
Sits at the end of the Alameda de los Descalzos, laid out in 1611
Museum since 1981, with 300-plus Cusco, Lima and Quito school paintings
Library holds over 15,000 vellum-bound volumes
w: Barefoot Franciscans built this retreat in 1595 at the far end of a poplar promenade, a place to pray away from the city's noise. Inside hang 300-odd colonial canvases and a library of 15,000 goatskin books, plus cells, refectory and a pharmacy kept as the friars left them. Rimac at its most hushed.

hook: Baroque water promenade built to court an actress
facts: Commissioned by Viceroy Amat between 1770 and 1776
Modelled on the water gardens of Narbonne in France
Arches, oculi and a reflecting pool restored in 2015
Linked to the actress Micaela Villegas, La Perricholi
legend: Legend says Amat raised it to woo La Perricholi, who asked him to lay the moon at her feet
w: Viceroy Amat poured French taste and diverted river water into this baroque parade of arches and pools from 1770, never quite finishing it. Legend says he built the lot to flatter the actress La Perricholi, who teased that she would love him once he laid the moon at her feet. He gave her a mirror pond instead.

hook: A record-breaking circuit of thirteen lit fountains
facts: Set inside the 1929 Parque de la Reserva, reopened as fountains in 2007
Holds a Guinness record as the largest fountain complex in a park
Thirteen fountains, including a 80-metre water jet
Evening shows project light, lasers and music onto the spray
w: By day a sober 1929 park; after dark, thirteen fountains light up and throw water 80 metres skyward, earning a Guinness record for the largest of its kind. One tunnel of spray you can walk through; another screens films onto a wall of water. Civic spectacle at full, unembarrassed blast.

hook: Paifang gate to the Americas' oldest Chinatown
facts: Paifang arch raised in 1971 at Jiron Ucayali and Andahuaylas
Eight metres high and thirteen wide, flanked by stone lions since 2017
Capon street block was the heart of 19th-century coolie-era Lima
Confucian inscription reads that under one sky all are equal
w: Chinese labourers shipped to coastal plantations in the 1850s drifted to this block of Calle Capon, building the oldest Chinatown in the Americas around their kitchens. A scarlet paifang, raised in 1971 and now guarded by stone lions, frames it. Its motto: under one sky, all are equal. Then lunch.

hook: Riverside park built over Lima's old defensive wall
facts: Opened 2004 along the left bank of the Rimac river
Exposes a 300-metre stretch of the 1684-1687 city wall
Wall once ran where the avenues Alfonso Ugarte and Grau now lie
Small site museum displays finds dug from the ground
w: Lima ringed itself with a great adobe wall from 1684 to keep pirates out, then tore most of it down as the city outgrew it. This riverside park, opened in 2004, uncovers a 300-metre run of the old rampart beside the Rimac, with the foundations laid bare. The edge of the colonial town, made visible.

hook: Fashion photographer's museum in a Barranco casona
facts: Founded by Peruvian photographer Mario Testino in 2012
Set in an 1898 adobe republican mansion on Pedro de Osma
Permanent room shows Testino's Alta Moda portraits of Cusco dress
Has shown his portraits of Diana, Princess of Wales
w: Lima-born Mario Testino, who shot Diana and the glossy magazines, brought his archive home in 2012 to a low adobe mansion of 1898 in Barranco. The keeper room holds Alta Moda: highland Peruvians in full traditional dress, photographed with the same gloss as any cover star. Vanity, redirected.

hook: Roofless seaside chapel born of a fishermen's miracle
facts: Mid-18th-century chapel beside the Puente de los Suspiros
Once Barranco's parish church, shut since the 1974 earthquake
Roofless shell long fenced off, with restoration slated to begin
Traditionally held as the origin of the Barranco district
legend: Legend says lost fishermen were guided ashore by a glowing cross, and built the chapel in thanks
w: Legend says two fishermen, lost in fog off this cliff, rowed to safety by the light of a luminous cross and raised a chapel where it had shone, the seed of Barranco itself. The 1974 quake unroofed it, and it has stood fenced and skeletal ever since, beside the Bridge of Sighs. A ruin built on rescue.

hook: Church grown around a quake-proof wall painting
facts: Holds the original Senor de los Milagros image on an adobe wall
Image reputedly painted around 1651 by an Angolan-born man
Wall survived the great Lima earthquakes of 1655 and 1687
Sets off Peru's largest religious procession each October
legend: Legend says the painted wall alone stood unscathed as quakes levelled all around it
w: Around 1651 an enslaved man from Angola painted a dark-skinned Christ on a humble adobe wall. When the quakes of 1655 and 1687 flattened the quarter, that wall, they say, stood untouched. A whole church rose around it, and each October a million-strong procession carries its copy through Lima. Paint that would not fall.
